Recent Advances in Aqueous Zinc-Ion Batteries
Guozhao Fang,\(^{\dagger}\) Jiang Zhou,\(^{*,\dagger, \ddagger}\) Anqiang Pan,\(^{\dagger, \ddagger}\) and Shuquan Liang\(^{*,\dagger, \ddagger}\)
†School of Materials Science and Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, PR China
\(^{\ddagger}\)Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Materials Science and Engineering, Ministry of Education, Central South University, Changsha 410083, PR China

ABSTRACT: Although current high-energy-density lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have taken over the commercial rechargeable battery market, increasing concerns about limited lithium resources, high cost, and insecurity of organic electrolyte scale-up limit their further development. Rechargeable aqueous zinc-ion batteries (ZIBs), an alternative battery chemistry, have paved the way not only for realizing environmentally benign and safe energy storage devices but also for reducing the manufacturing costs of next-generation batteries. This Review underscores recent advances in aqueous ZIBs; these include the design of a highly reversible Zn anode, optimization of the electrolyte, and a wide range of cathode materials and their energy storage mechanisms. We also present recent advanced techniques that aim at overcoming the current
issues in aqueous ZIB systems. This Review on the future perspectives and research directions will provide a guide for future aqueous ZIB study.

electrochemical energy storage devices, such as rechargeable batteries, are critical for overcoming the worldwide energy challenge. Because of their high energy density and long-cycle-life, nonaqueous lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have dominated in portable electronics and emerging electric/hybrid vehicles and now are considered as the possible choice for future electric vehicles and grid-scale energy storage systems. However, the increasing concerns about limited lithium resources, high-cost, and safety issue strongly limit their further development for large-scale applications. Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) and potassium-ion batteries (KIBs) are plausible alternatives to LIBs because these devices are based on relatively abundant and cheap sodium (potassium) elements and their similar chemical properties to lithium, but they suffer from the low energy density, use highly toxic and flammable electrolytes, and have high operating cost and security issues. The drawbacks of these organic-based systems motivate us to explore alternative battery chemistry with low-cost, high safety, and long cycle life.

Aqueous rechargeable batteries are a promising class of batteries for grid-scale electrochemical energy storage because of their low-cost, high operational safety, and environmental benignity. Furthermore, the aqueous electrolytes offer 2 orders of magnitude higher ionic conductivities \((\sim 1\mathrm{Scm}^{-1})\) than that of nonaqueous electrolytes \((\sim 1 - 10\mathrm{mScm}^{-1})\) To date, a variety of aqueous batteries based on naturally abundant alkali metal cations (e.g., \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) and \(\mathrm{K}^+\) )[9,10] and multivalent charge carriers (e.g., \(\mathrm{Mg}^{2 + }\) \(\mathrm{Al}^{3 + }\) ,and \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2 + }\) ) are under development.[11]

Obviously, the systems employing multivalent ions can, in principle, achieve higher specific capacity and energy density because of the multiple electrons involved in redox reactions.\(^{12,13}\) The development of aqueous magnesium-ion batteries (MIBs) is restricted because the available cathode materials for this system have been limited to very few compounds owing to the sluggish \(\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\) diffusion in host lattices.\(^{14}\) In addition, the passivation of the \(\mathrm{Mg}\) anode greatly prevents further transport of \(\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\) ions, requiring a usable anode material to build more powerful aqueous MIBs.\(^{11,15,16}\) Aqueous aluminum-ion batteries (AIBs) have also been proposed because of the high volumetric capacity (ca. \(8040\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{cm}^{-3}\)) and good gravimetric capacity (ca. \(2980\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\)) of the aluminum anode.\(^{17-19}\) Nonetheless, because of the formation of a protective oxide (\(\mathrm{Al}_2\mathrm{O}_3\)) film on the anode in aqueous electrolytes (\(4 < \mathrm{pH} < 8\)) accompanied by a decrease in cell efficiency and electrode potential, as well as the tendency of aluminum to corrode unevenly,\(^{19-21}\) aqueous AIBs are still in the experimental stage and are limited to the development of primary high-energy systems.

In this regard, aqueous zinc-ion batteries (ZIBs) with mild neutral pH (or slightly acidic) electrolyte hold particular promise for grid-scale energy storage.[22] Since Volta et al. employed metallic zinc \((\mathrm{Zn})\) in the first battery in 1799, Zn anode has been

To date, many significant works regarding aqueous ZIBs have been reported in the literature, including metallic zinc \((\mathrm{Zn})\) anode\(^{31,32}\) and cathode materials (e.g., manganese-based oxides,\(^{22,33-35}\) Prussian blue analogs,\(^{36,37}\) vanadium-based oxides,\(^{38-40}\) polyanion compounds,\(^{41,42}\) Chevrel phase compounds,\(^{43,44}\) sustainable quinone analogs,\(^{45,46}\) etc.). Recently, Fan et al. summarized part of the cathode material and their reaction mechanisms as well as their electrolytes.\(^{47}\) In this Review, the energy storage mechanisms in these systems are systematically analyzed and summarized. Furthermore, the merits and issues as well as the optimization strategies related to various cathodes, \(\mathrm{Zn}\) anode, and aqueous electrolytes are discussed. Finally, some potential future research directions from our personal perspectives or other related research topics are also presented. This Review provides a comprehensive overview focusing on the recent progress, challenges, and future perspectives in aqueous ZIBs.

Energy Storage Mechanisms in Aqueous ZIB Systems. Unlike the well-established lithium/sodium-ion-based energy storage chemistries (e.g., insertion-conversion-alloying reaction mechanism) for storage monovalent alkali metal cations,[48] the reaction mechanisms in aqueous ZIB systems are complicated and controversial. To date, the reaction mechanisms involved remain a topic of discussion and are underdeveloped. According to the literature, the redox reactions in aqueous ZIB systems mainly involve three mechanisms: \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) insertion/ extraction,[22,38,49] chemical conversion reaction,[50] and \(\mathrm{H}^{+}/\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) insertion/extraction,[51,52] which are summarized and discussed in this section.

\(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) Insertion/Extraction Mechanism. Many compounds with tunnel-type and layered-type structure enable the insertion/ extraction of \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) ions into/from their hosts owing to the small ionic radii of \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) (0.74 Å). For example, Kang et al. reported an aqueous ZIB, which is composed of an \(\alpha\text{-MnO}_2\) cathode, a zinc anode, and a mild \(\mathrm{ZnSO}_4\) or \(\mathrm{Zn(NO_3)_2}\) aqueous electrolyte (Figure 1A),[22] demonstrating that the charge storage mechanism in the aqueous \(\mathrm{Zn}/\alpha\text{-MnO}_2\) system is based on the migration of \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) ions between tunnels of \(\alpha\text{-MnO}_2\) cathode and \(\mathrm{Zn}\) anode, namely a typical \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) insertion/extraction reaction mechanism as follows:

Soon afterward, Kang's group further revealed \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) insertion into the host of \(\alpha\)-, \(\beta\)-, \(\gamma\)-, and \(\delta\)-type \(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) with different tunnel structures as their main charge storage mechanism.[53]

It should be noted that some studies also focused on \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) insertion into the \(\alpha\text{-MnO}_2\) host, but they demonstrated a phase transition from tunneled structure (\(\alpha\text{-MnO}_2\)) to layered Zn-buserite or birnessite,[54,55] not the spinel \(\mathrm{ZnMn}_2\mathrm{O}_4\) phase mentioned above. Kim et al. reported an aqueous \(\mathrm{Zn}/\gamma\text{-MnO}_2\) cell in which \(\gamma\text{-MnO}_2\) undergoes a complex multiphase transformation on successive insertion of \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\).[49] As shown in Figure 1B, in the early stages, partial \(\gamma\text{-MnO}_2\) is transferred into spinel-type \(\mathrm{ZnMn}_2\mathrm{O}_4\). The further inserted \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) ions occupy the \(1\times 2\) tunnels of \(\gamma\text{-MnO}_2\) to form a tunnel-type \(\gamma\text{-Zn}_x\mathrm{MnO}_2\) phase. As more \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) ions are inserted, Zn-containing tunnels in a portion of \(\gamma\text{-Zn}_x\mathrm{MnO}_2\) tend to expand and open up the structural framework to form a layered-type L-Zn\(_y\mathrm{MnO}_2\) phase. For the \(\beta\text{-MnO}_2\), it seems to hardly incorporate \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) ions because of its narrow \(1\times 1\) tunnels;[53] however, Chen et al. elucidated that a layer-type B-\(\mathrm{Zn}_x\mathrm{MnO}_2\)-\(n\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\) phase was generated during the initial discharge, followed by reversible insertion/extraction of \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) ions in this layered structure (Figure 1C).[56] In some of them, the insertion/extraction of \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) is homogeneous (e.g., \(\alpha\)-, and \(\beta\text{-MnO}_2\)), while in others, it is heterogeneous (e.g., \(\gamma\text{-MnO}_2\)). The different inserted behaviors of \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) ions in these \(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) may be attributed to the difference in crystallographic polymorphs that change the ion insertion thermodynamics and kinetics.[57] In addition to \(\mathrm{MnO}_2\), other manganese-based oxides have also been investigated. For instance, Chen et al. reported a cation-deficient spinel \(\mathrm{ZnMn}_2\mathrm{O}_4\) cathode with

highly reversible insertion/extraction of \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\).33 The ex situ XRD patterns were conducted at different charge/discharge states to elucidate the evolution of \(\mathrm{ZnMn_2O_4}\) cathode. As shown in Figure 1D, the diffraction peaks gradually shift to higher angle when charged from 0.8 to \(1.9\mathrm{V}\), demonstrating the shrink of lattice spacing after the extraction of \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\). As expected, the diffraction peaks are recovered upon full discharging back to \(0.8\mathrm{V}\), suggesting good reversibility of the \(\mathrm{ZnMn_2O_4}\) cathode.

In addition to the progress of manganese-based oxides, other materials with \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) insertion reaction mechanism are also being intensively studied. Nazar et al. opened a new chapter in the \(\mathrm{Zn}/\) vanadium-based oxides battery, which is constructed by a \(\mathrm{Zn}_{0.25}\mathrm{V}_2\mathrm{O}_5\cdot n\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\) cathode with a \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) (de)intercalation mechanism.[38] At the initial state, the structure of pristine \(\mathrm{Zn}_{0.25}\mathrm{V}_2\mathrm{O}_5\cdot n\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\) was modified via water intercalation upon immersion in the electrolyte, which results in an increase in the interlayer distance, thus expanding the galleries for facile \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) intercalation. As the discharge/charge reaction proceeds, the insertion/extraction of \(1.1\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) ions into/from \(\mathrm{Zn}_{0.25}\mathrm{V}_2\mathrm{O}_5\cdot n\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\) is highly reversible, corresponding to the following equation:

Since then, a series of vanadium-based materials, such as \(\mathrm{V}_2\mathrm{O}_5\cdot n\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O},\) [39,58] \(\mathrm{Zn_3V_2O_7(OH)_2\cdot 2H_2O},\) [40] \(\mathrm{Ca_{0.25}V_2O_5\cdot nH_2O},\) [59] \(\mathrm{Na_2V_6O_{16}}\) [3H2O,[60,61] \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.33}\mathrm{V}_2\mathrm{O}_5,\) [62] VS,[63] etc., based on \(\mathrm{Zn^{2 + }}\) intercalation chemistry have been investigated. For example, Zhang et al. reported a layered \(\mathrm{V}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) cathode, which allows a high reversibility for hydrated \(\mathrm{Zn^{2 + }}\) (de)intercalation (Figure 2B).[58] It demonstrates that the coinjection of \(\mathrm{Zn^{2 + }}\) and \(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\) results in a structural evolution from layered \(\mathrm{V}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) to layered \(\mathrm{Zn_xV_2O_5}\) - \(n\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\), but a reverse structural evolution was observed during the extraction of hydrated \(\mathrm{Zn^{2 + }}\), indicating good structural reversibility. Very recently, Liang et al. demonstrated that different structures and configurations play a key role in the storage/ release behaviors of \(\mathrm{Zn^{2 + }}\) ions.[64,65] For instance, they constructed two typical sodium vanadates with \(\mathrm{NaV_3O_8}\) type layered structure, e.g., \(\mathrm{Na}_5\mathrm{V}_{12}\mathrm{O}_{32}\) \((\mathrm{Na}_{1.25}\mathrm{V}_3\mathrm{O}_8)\), \(\mathrm{HNaV_6O_{16}}\) [4H2O \((\mathrm{H}_{0.5}\mathrm{Na}_{0.5}\mathrm{V}_3\mathrm{O}_8\cdot 2\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O})]\), and \(\beta \text{-Na}_{0.33}\mathrm{V}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) type tunneled structure, e.g. \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.76}\mathrm{V}_6\mathrm{O}_{15}\) \((\mathrm{Na}_{0.253}\mathrm{V}_2\mathrm{O}_5)\), indicating that the structural destruction and the formation of the second phase were observed for the layered \(\mathrm{Na}_5\mathrm{V}_{12}\mathrm{O}_{32}\) and \(\mathrm{HNaV_6O_{16}}\) [4H2O], while the tunneled \(\mathrm{Na_{0.76}V_6O_{15}}\) exhibits reversible insertion/ extraction of \(\mathrm{Zn^{2 + }}\) without structural collapse (Figure 2C). It is reported that layered \(\mathrm{NaV_3O_8}\) possesses single-connected and triconnected oxygen atoms on the layer surface, while the tunneled \(\beta \text{-Na}_{0.33}\mathrm{V}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) contains only single-connected oxygen atoms that can provide stronger interaction with the \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) than

that of triconnected oxygen atoms.\(^{66,67}\) As a result, some part of \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) in \(\mathrm{Na}_5\mathrm{V}_{12}\mathrm{O}_{32}\), which bonds with triconnected oxygen atoms, is more likely to be displaced by the \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) to form a new phase of \(\mathrm{Zn}_4\mathrm{V}_2\mathrm{O}_9\). In contrast, the \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) in \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.76}\mathrm{V}_6\mathrm{O}_{15}\) bonding with single-connected oxygen atoms acts as strong "pillars" to stabilize the whole structure and make it more difficult to be destroyed.\(^{64}\)

Because of their open framework, Prussian blue analogues \((\mathrm{PBAs})^{36,37,68-71}\) and polyanionic olivine-based phosphates \(\mathrm{MNPO}_4\) (\(\mathrm{M} = \mathrm{Li}, \mathrm{Na}, \mathrm{K}; \mathrm{N} = \mathrm{V}, \mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Mn}, \mathrm{Ni}, \mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{etc.}\)) have been also verified to enable the insertion/extraction of divalent \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) ions. The cathode electrode reactions in these compounds involve the insertion/extraction of \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) into/from their three-dimensional pathways, and anode electrode reactions are related to the deposition and dissolution of zinc.

Chemical Conversion Reaction Mechanism. Unlike most cases in which the capacity is induced by the reversible \(\mathrm{Zn^{2 + }}\) insertion/extraction, Liu and co-workers demonstrated a \(\mathrm{Zn}/\) \(\alpha \text{-MnO}_2\) system, which operates on the principle of a chemical conversion reaction mechanism between \(\alpha \text{-MnO}_2\) and MnOOH.[50] As shown in Figure 3A, the MnOOH phase is observed at the fully discharged state, which is due to the reaction between \(\alpha \text{-MnO}_2\) with a proton from water. To reach a neutral charge in the system, the sequent \(\mathrm{OH}^-\) ions will react with \(\mathrm{ZnSO_4}\) and \(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\) in the aqueous electrolyte to form a \(\mathrm{ZnSO_4[Zn(OH)_2]_3\cdot xH_2O}\) phase (Figure 3B). This phenomenon is strongly supported by the STEM-EDS mapping of the \(\alpha \text{-MnO}_2\) electrode in the discharged state, which reveals that the short nanorods and nanoparticles consist of O and Mn (Figure 3C), while Zn is mainly distributed on the flakelike compounds (Figure 3D). As discussed above, several different mechanisms are observed for \(\alpha\text{-MnO}_2\) in different literature contributions, suggesting more precise experiments and analysis methods are required to further clarify the reaction mechanism of \(\alpha\text{-MnO}_2\).

\(\mathrm{H^{+}}\) and \(\mathrm{Zn^{2 + }}\) Insertion/Extraction Mechanism. The feasible host materials with open tunnel or layered framework may enable the coinjection of \(\mathrm{H^{+}}\) and \(\mathrm{Zn^{2 + }}\). Because of the different insertion thermodynamics and kinetics of \(\mathrm{H^{+}}\) and \(\mathrm{Zn^{2 + }}\), Wang et al. proposed a consequent \(\mathrm{H^{+}}\) and \(\mathrm{Zn^{2 + }}\) insertion reaction mechanism for aqueous \(\mathrm{Zn} / \alpha \text{-MnO}_2\) battery.[51] These two reactions happened in two regions, which are identified by the electrochemical and structural analysis. Discharge galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) profiles of the \(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) electrode (Figure 4A) show that the total overvoltage in region II is \(0.6\mathrm{V}\), which is much larger than that in region I \((0.08\mathrm{V})\). The significant difference in regions I and II should be attributed to different ion insertion. It is known that the voltage jump observed instantly after applying current is attributed to the ohm and charge transfer resistances, while the gradual voltage change after the discharge is mainly ascribed to the ion diffusion. The large overvoltage in region II is attributed to both large voltage jump and slow ion diffusion. Given the large size of \(\mathrm{Zn^{2 + }}\) and the fact that bivalent \(\mathrm{Zn^{2 + }}\) would exhibit strong electrostatic interactions with the host lattice, it is highly possible that the voltage plateau in region II

is mainly attributed to the slow \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) insertion. This hypothesis is confirmed by the electrochemical behaviors of \(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) electrode in \(0.2\mathrm{M}\) \(\mathrm{MnSO}_4\) electrolytes with or without \(\mathrm{ZnSO}_4\). As shown in Figure 4B, the discharged plateau at \(1.3\mathrm{V}\) in region II disappeared because of the absence of \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\). A typical \(\mathrm{MnOOH}\) phase is observed after discharge to \(1.3\mathrm{V}\) because of the \(\mathrm{H}^+\) insertion, while the \(\mathrm{ZnMn}_2\mathrm{O}_4\) phase can be observed only after further discharging to \(1.0\mathrm{V}\) (Figure 4C), which strongly proved that the main reaction in region II is contributed by the \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) insertion.

The coinsertion of \(\mathrm{H^{+}}\) and \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2 + }\) mechanism is also observed in the vanadium-based oxides \((\mathrm{NaV}_{3}\mathrm{O}_{8}\cdot 1.5\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O},\) NVO) as proposed by Chen and co-workers.52 As depicted by ex situ XRD patterns (Figure 4D), the \(\mathrm{Zn_4SO_4(OH)_6\cdot 4H_2O}\) phase (JCPDS: 39-688) is successively formed during the discharge process, which is due to \(\mathrm{OH^{-}}\) reacting with \(\mathrm{ZnSO_4}\) and \(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\) in the aqueous electrolyte. To reach a neutral charge system, the extra \(\mathrm{H^{+}}\) would move into the NVO host to balance the electron-rich condition. The ex situ solid-state \(^1\mathrm{H}\) NMR analyses were conducted to confirm the continuous insertion of \(\mathrm{H^{+}}\) in the NVO during the discharge process (Figure 4E). Compared with the initial state, an extra peak at \(2.7~\mathrm{ppm}\) at the selected charge/discharge states would be ascribed to the \(\mathrm{H^{+}}\) that inserted in the NVO. In addition to the reversible insertion of \(\mathrm{H^{+}}\) ,the Zn 2p spectra of NVO electrodes (Figure 4F) exhibit three pairs of \(\mathrm{Zn^{2 + }}\) peaks located at 1023/1046, 1024/ 1047, and \(1025 / 1048\mathrm{eV}\) which are assigned to the inserted \(\mathrm{Zn^{2 + }}\) in NVO, as well as the \(\mathrm{Zn^{2 + }}\) in \(\mathrm{Zn(OH)_2}\) and \(\mathrm{ZnSO_4}\) from \(\mathrm{Zn_4SO_4(OH)_6\cdot 4H_2O}\) respectively,52 indicating that \(\mathrm{Zn^{2 + }}\) takes part in the energy storage in the \(\mathrm{Zn / NaV_{3}O_{8}\cdot 1.5H_{2}O}\) system.

Different Types and Challenges of Cathode Materials. Challenges still exist in aqueous ZIBs to achieve great breakthroughs in practical applications. One of the major challenges in the development and application of aqueous ZIBs is the lack of suitable cathodes that can offer high capacity and good structure stability during the process of guest ion storage.

As a result, most of the literature contributions on the study of aqueous ZIBs are focused on exploring and developing cathode materials. At present, these cathode materials mainly include (1) manganese-based oxides, (2) vanadium-based oxides, (3) Prussian blue analogues, (4) olivine-based phosphates, (5) Chevrel phase compounds, and (6) sustainable quinone cathodes. Others like \(\mathrm{VS}_2\), \(^{63}\mathrm{Co(III)}\) rich-\(\mathrm{Co}_3\mathrm{O}_4\), etc. have also been explored as high-performance aqueous ZIB cathodes. The features of these species, such as energy storage mechanism, testing voltage, operating voltage, specific discharge capacity, and electrolyte used in aqueous ZIB systems, are summarized in Table 1. The relationship between operating voltage and specific capacity of these cathodes is shown visually in Figure 5. The merits and challenges of these cathode materials are also discussed in this section.

Manganese-Based Oxides. Manganese (Mn)-based oxides, which possess unique advantages, such as low-cost, abundant reserves, environmental friendliness, low toxicity, and multiple valence states \((\mathrm{Mn}^{0},\mathrm{Mn}^{2 + },\mathrm{Mn}^{3 + },\mathrm{Mn}^{4 + }\) and \(\mathrm{Mn}^{7 + })\) , have been considered as attractive energy storage materials.[76-79] Recently, manganese-based oxides including \(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) with different crystal structures,[22,50,51,53,80-82] \(\mathrm{Mn}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3},^{35}\mathrm{Mn}_{3}\mathrm{O}_{4},^{83,84}\) \(\mathrm{ZnMn_2O_4},^{33}\) etc., are one of the most promising cathodes for aqueous ZIBs.

In the early state of aqueous ZIBs, extensive research efforts have been focused on \(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) for cathode materials because their tunnel or layered structure allows reversible insertion/ extraction of \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) ions. The family of \(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) materials have a variety of crystallographic polymorphs (e.g., \(\alpha\), \(\beta\), \(\gamma\), \(\delta\), \(\varepsilon\), and \(\lambda\)-type \(\mathrm{MnO}_2\)), which are dependent on the type of linkage between the fundamental octahedral unit \([\mathrm{MnO}_6]\). The basic structural unit of \(\alpha\)-\(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) is composed of \([\mathrm{MnO}_6]\) octahedral double chains that are corner-sharing with adjacent double chains in the plane and form \((2 \times 2) + (1 \times 1)\) tunnel structures (Figure 6A), which can accommodate different cations or coordination ions. The previous reported \(\alpha\)-\(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) cathodes displayed the similar working voltages around \(1.2 \sim 1.4\mathrm{~V}\) with a capacity of more than \(200\mathrm{~mA~h~g^{-1}}\). Among all the \(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) polymorphs, \(\beta\)-\(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) structure is regarded as the most thermodynamically stable structure, which is composed of \([\mathrm{MnO}_6]\) octahedral single chains by vertex-sharing, forming \((1 \times 1)\) tunnel along \(c\) axis (Figure 6B). This narrow tunnel is usually not conducive to the diffusion of cations e.g. \(\mathrm{Li^{+}}\) and \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\). Recently, however, Chen et al. found that \(\beta\)-\(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) enabled a reversible insertion/extraction of \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) ions through a phase transition to form a layer-type phase \(\mathrm{Zn}\)-buserite during the initial discharge. Remarkably, the cathode exhibits a high reversible capacity of \(225\mathrm{~mA~h~g^{-1}}\) and long-term cyclability with \(94\%\) capacity retention over 2000 cycles. According to Kim, the \(\gamma\)-\(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) phase with an irregular \((1 \times 2)\) tunnel (Figure 6C) can deliver a high capacity of \(285\mathrm{~mA~h~g^{-1}}\) at \(0.05\mathrm{~mA~cm^{-2}}\) with a defined plateau at around \(1.25\mathrm{~V}\) vs \(\mathrm{Zn} / \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\). As a typical layered compound, \(\delta\)-\(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) (Figure 6D) is also investigated to be a potential cathode for aqueous ZIBs with a high capacity of \(269\mathrm{~mA~h~g^{-1}}\) at \(100\mathrm{~mA~g^{-1}}\) using \(1\mathrm{~mol~L^{-1}~ZnSO_4}\) as electrolyte. In addition, the \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) storage capability of \(\varepsilon\)-\(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) has been studied by Wang and co-workers. It is clear that the electrochemical behavior of \(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) strongly depends on its crystallographic forms.

However, as mentioned earlier, the most critical issue of \(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) is the energy storage mechanisms involved in \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) insertion/extraction,[22,54] chemical conversion reaction,[50] and \(\mathrm{H}^{+}/\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) insertion/extraction,[51] which are quite complicated

and need to be further clarified. Another major problem is the significant capacity fading of these \(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) materials due to dissolution of \(\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}\) from \(\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}\) disproportionation into the electrolyte during cycling.[50] As evidenced by Liu et al., a rapid deterioration in capacity was observed for the \(\mathrm{Zn/MnO}_2\) battery upon cycling in \(2\mathrm{M}\) \(\mathrm{ZnSO}_4\) (Figure 6E).[50] Elemental analysis of dissolved \(\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}\) ions shows that the \(\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}\) ions gradually dissolved in the electrolyte but slowed after the initial 10 cycles. This is probably because the \(\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}\) dissolution from the \(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) electrode increases \(\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}\) concentration in the electrolyte and thus suppresses significant continuous \(\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}\) dissolution. Therefore, preaddition of a certain amount of \(\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}\) ions will provide an appropriate equilibrium between \(\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}\) dissolution and the reoxidation of \(\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}\) in the electrolyte, leading to high stability of the electrode. This method is confirmed to work in many cases, such as \(\mathrm{ZnSO}_4\)-based electrolyte (Figure 6F)[50] and \(\mathrm{Zn(CF_3SO_3)_2}\)-based electrolyte (Figure 6G).[56]

Except for the \(\mathrm{MnO}_2\), other manganese-based oxides have also shown good Zn storage capability as cathode materials. For instance, Chen et al. reported a cation-deficient spinel \(\mathrm{ZnMn}_2\mathrm{O}_4\) cathode for aqueous ZIBs using \(\mathrm{Zn(CF_3SO_3)_2}\) electrolyte.[33] It is reported that the ideal spinel structure is unfit for \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) insertion because of the high electrostatic repulsion among \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) cations within the lattice.[87] Introducing abundant Mn vacancies in spinel \(\mathrm{ZnMn}_2\mathrm{O}_4\) is verified to be a peculiar merit for \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) diffusion in the spinel framework.[33] In a perfect spinel without Mn vacancies, \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) ions migrate from one tetrahedral site (4a) to another by passing through an unoccupied octahedral site (8c), which is strongly impeded by a large electrostatic repulsion from Mn cations in a neighboring octahedral site (8d) (Figure 7A). However, the spinel rich in Mn vacancies allows for easier diffusion of \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) without much electrostatic barrier, leading to higher diffusion coefficient of \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) and consequently faster electrode kinetics (Figure 7B). In addition, Kang et al. enriched the manganese-based cathode family material by exploring two manganese oxides (e.g., \(\alpha\text{-Mn}_2\mathrm{O}_3\)[35] and \(\mathrm{Mn}_3\mathrm{O}_4\)[83]). Following work by Liang et al. demonstrated that combining \(\mathrm{Mn}_3\mathrm{O}_4\) with three-dimensional (3D) conductive substrates can improve its rate capability and long-term cycling performance (Figure 7C).[84] Very recently, Kim et al. developed a new manganese-based cathode \(\mathrm{MgMn}_2\mathrm{O}_4\) with high voltage and high energy (Figure 7D).[88] The results indicate that partial \(\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\) ions escape from the spinel during the initial charge process, while the stable cointercalation of \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}/\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\) into the spinel was observed in the subsequent cycles, strongly supporting the conclusion that the electrochemical extraction/insertion of cations into the spinel is reversible.

Prussian Blue Analogs. Prussian blue analogues (PBAs) exhibit an open-framework structure, in which the \(\mathrm{Fe(III)C_6}\) octahedra are linked to \(\mathrm{M(II)N_4}\) (\(\mathrm{M} = \mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\), \(\mathrm{Ni^{2+}}\), \(\mathrm{Cu^{2+}}\), etc.) tetrahedra via CN ligands to form a porous 3D framework with large open A sites (Figure 8A). Such a framework endows PBAs with the possibility of behaving as intercalation hosts for various cations. Many studies focus on the PBA family as hosts for monovalent or divalent ion batteries in both aqueous and organic solutions.

Recently, Zhang et al. reported a zinc hexacyanoferrate \((\mathrm{Zn}_{3}[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}]_{2},\) ZnHCF) as cathode in aqueous ZIB systems using \(\mathrm{ZnSO_4}\) electrolyte. \(^{36}\) ZnHCF yields a higher \(\mathrm{Zn^{2 + }}\) intercalation voltage leading to an average operation voltage of ca. \(1.7\mathrm{V}\) when combined with a zinc anode (Figure 8B).

High operating voltage is desirable to improve the energy density of the battery. Other PBAs such as copper hexacyanoferrate\(^{37,68,69}\) also exhibited a high operating voltage, for instance, a cathodic peak at \(\sim 0.6\) V and a corresponding anodic peak at \(\sim 0.8\) V vs SHE (Figure 8C), with a reversible insertion/extraction of \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) ions into/from the A sites of PBAs crystal lattice structure. However, the serious issue is that most of these materials deliver an extremely low capacity of less than \(70~\mathrm{mA~h~g^{-1}}\) (Figure 8D), leading to insufficient energy density, which greatly restricts their further development.

Vanadium-Based Oxides. Owing to the low cost, abundant resources, and multivalence of vanadium, vanadium oxides and their derivatives are the most important compounds being used in the field of battery systems, particularly in LIBs and SIBs.[93-99] In recent years, research has been undertaken for aqueous ZIBs as well, wherein metal vanadates were tested as a promising cathode. Nazar et al. constructed a layered vanadium oxide bronze \((\mathrm{Zn}_{0.25}\mathrm{V}_2\mathrm{O}_5\cdot n\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O})\) [38] in which interlayer metal ions \((\mathrm{Zn}^{2+})\) and structural water act as pillars to stabilize the structure during cycling (Figure 9A). The \(\mathrm{Zn}/\mathrm{Zn}_{0.25}\mathrm{V}_2\mathrm{O}_5\cdot n\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\) cell delivered a very high capacity of \(282\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) at \(300\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) with an average operating voltage of \(\sim 0.9\mathrm{V}\) (Figure 9B). This means the cell can display a specific energy of \(\sim 250\mathrm{Wh}\mathrm{kg}^{-1}\) (cathode only). The structural water

molecules are revealed to play a crucial role by reversibly expanding and contracting the interlayer spacing of \(\mathrm{Zn_{0.25}V_2O_5}\) that facilitates \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\) ingress/egress, thus resulting in good kinetics and high rate performance.\(^{38}\) The effect of structural water on \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\) storage performance is further demonstrated in hydrated \(\mathrm{V}_2\mathrm{O}_5\cdot n\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\) reported by Mai and Yang.\(^{39}\) The solid-state magic-angle-spinning (MAS) \({}^{1}\mathrm{H}\) NMR is conducted to explore structural evolution and the effect of structural water in the electrode materials (Figure 9C). The \({}^{1}\mathrm{H}\) spectrum of pristine hydrated \(\mathrm{V}_2\mathrm{O}_5\cdot n\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\) (black line) shows the broad resonance at \(5.6~\mathrm{ppm}\), which is attributed to the structural water trapped between \(\mathrm{V}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) bilayers. After charging to \(1.3~\mathrm{V}\), \({}^{1}\mathrm{H}\) peak (blue lines) shifts slightly upward to \(5.3~\mathrm{ppm}\) with an increased signal intensity accompanied with the decrease in the \(\mathrm{V}_2\mathrm{O}_5\cdot n\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\) interlayer distance from 12.6 to \(10.4\AA\). As evidenced by the authors, this can be explained by the formation of hydrogen bonds among \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\), \(\mathrm{CF_3SO_3}\), \(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\), and lattice oxygen that pull \(\mathrm{V}_2\mathrm{O}_5\cdot n\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\) bilayers closer. After discharging to \(0.2\mathrm{V}\), \({}^{1}\mathrm{H}\) peak (red lines) splits into two peaks. One is a relatively sharp peak at \(5.1~\mathrm{ppm}\) that is attributed to water molecules in the few remaining clusters of \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\), \(\mathrm{CF_3SO_3}\), and \(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\), while a relatively broad peak at \(2.9~\mathrm{ppm}\) is ascribed to the water molecules associated with the inserted \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\), thus increasing bilayer distance from 10.4 to \(13.5\AA\) (Figure 9C).

It seems that these water molecules effectively function like a "lubricant" to facilitate fast \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) transportation. Moreover, the charge shield effect of the crystal \(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\) molecules could reduce the effective charges of inserted \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) ions and thus enhance the capacity and rate performance.

Except for structural water, electrolyte optimization may also be a promising method to improve the electrochemical performance for vanadium-based cathodes. Chen et al. found that aqueous \(\mathrm{ZnSO_4}\) electrolyte could lead to the fast dissolution of \(\mathrm{NaV}_3\mathrm{O}_8\cdot 1.5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\) as well as the formation of vertical and harsh Zn dendrites, thus resulting in a rapid degradation in capacity (Figure 10A). The addition of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) into the electrolyte can change the dissolution equilibrium of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) from

\(\mathrm{NaV}_3\mathrm{O}_8\cdot 1.5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\) electrodes and thus impede the continuous dissolution of \(\mathrm{NaV}_3\mathrm{O}_8\cdot 1.5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\), as supported by the observation that the electrolyte (1 M \(\mathrm{ZnSO}_4\) and 1 M \(\mathrm{Na_2SO_4}\)) is transparent and colorless even when the \(\mathrm{NaV}_3\mathrm{O}_8\cdot 1.5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\) electrode is dipped into the electrolyte for \(24\mathrm{h}\) (inset in Figure 10A), indicating that the dissolution of \(\mathrm{NaV}_3\mathrm{O}_8\cdot 1.5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\) was suppressed. As a result, the capacity of a \(\mathrm{Zn} // \mathrm{NaV}_3\mathrm{O}_8\cdot 1.5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\) battery with \(1\mathrm{M}\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{SO}_4\) additive can stabilize at \(221\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) after 100 cycles with a retention rate of \(90\%\) at a current density of \(1\mathrm{A}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) (Figure 10B).

Polyanion Compounds. In the past decade, extensive research efforts have been focused on polyanion compounds as cathode materials in both aqueous and nonaqueous LIBs or SIBs because of their open channels for diffusion of \(\mathrm{Li^{+}}\) and \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\). [113-115] Recently, polyanion compounds have been applied in aqueous ZIBs as reported in several contributions, wherein NASICON-type \(\mathrm{M}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\) (\(\mathrm{M} = \mathrm{Li}\), Na) have attracted considerable interest because of their thermodynamically stable structure. [41,72,73] Although the radius of the \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\) ion is \(0.74\AA\), which is smaller than that of the \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ion (\(0.99\AA\)), the cation radius would not be the only factor under consideration during the insertion of \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\) in aqueous ZIBs because the high polarization of the \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\) ion may limit its migration dynamics. [41]

Huang et al. developed a NASICON structured \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\) as cathode material for aqueous ZIBs using \(0.5\mathrm{mol}\mathrm{L}^{-1}\) \(\mathrm{Zn(CH_3COO)_2}\). They found a large voltage difference between the first and second charge curves for \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\) cathode, as

shown in Figure 11A, which is confirmed to be derived from the difference of chemical potential between \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\) and \(\mathrm{Zn}_x\mathrm{NaV}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\). The first oxidation peak at about \(1.46\mathrm{~V}\) corresponds to \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) deintercalation from \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\). However, the redox pair peaks during subsequent cycles appear at around \(1.05\) and \(1.28\mathrm{~V}\), which can be attributed to the deintercalation/intercalation of \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) from/into the \(\mathrm{NaV}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\) host. This demonstrated that during the discharge process, a \(\mathrm{Zn}\)-inserted phase \(\mathrm{Zn}_x\mathrm{NaV}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\), which has a redox potential lower than that of \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\). Preadding the \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) could suppress the formation of \(\mathrm{Zn}_x\mathrm{NaV}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\) phase, as demonstrated by Huang's further work.[72] The \(\mathrm{Zn}/\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\) cell with a hybrid aqueous electrolyte of \(0.5\mathrm{~mol~L}^{-1}\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{COONa}/\) \(\mathrm{Zn(CH_3COO)_2}\) exhibits almost overlapped cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves for the initial three cycles with a redox couple at about \(1.37\) and \(1.52\mathrm{~V}\) (Figure 11B), demonstrating the excellent structural reversibility of \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\). This electrochemical behavior for NASICON-type compounds is further verified by Zhao's work,[73] which exhibits only \(\mathrm{Li}^+\) insertion/ extraction into/from both the \(\mathrm{Li}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\) and \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\) cathodes in a hybrid aqueous electrolyte (\(1\mathrm{M}\) \(\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{SO}_4/2\mathrm{M}\) \(\mathrm{ZnSO_4}\)) (Figure 11C). Nonetheless, more evidence is required to determine how \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) is suppressed by \(\mathrm{Li}^+\) or \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) to enter the host of the NASICON-type structure.

Sustainable Quinone Cathodes. To date, great efforts have been made on building high-performance ZIBs using inorganic compounds and Prussian blue analogs, as discussed above. Because they are lightweight, environmentally friendly, and sustainable, organic compounds are also attractive for electrochemical energy storage devices;[119,120] especially, they have shown great superiority in both monovalent \(\left(\mathrm{Li}^{+}\right.\)and \(\mathrm{Na}^{+}\)) and divalent \(\left(\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\right)\) cation storage.[121-124] Among them, quinones, which are ubiquitous in nature, can act as sustainable and green electrode materials. However, currently most battery systems using quinone electrodes work with organic electrolytes,

which suffer from the fast capacity fading due to the dissolution of active materials.\(^{119}\) Applying quinone electrodes in aqueous batteries should be a smart choice as they can stably exist in water, which have been proven to have versatile properties in aqueous \(\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{Li}^{+}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{K}^{+}, \text{and} \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\right)\) rechargeable batteries.\(^{125}\)

Very recently, Chen and co-workers reported a series of quinone compounds as high-performance cathodes in aqueous ZIBs, in which the calix[4]quinone (C4Q) with open bowl structures and eight carbonyls can provide reversible and efficient \(\mathrm{Zn}\) storage. The C4Q cathode displays a flat and safe operation voltage of \(1.0\mathrm{V}\) with a low polarization of \(70\mathrm{mV}\), which contributes to the stable energy output, and thus acquires a capacity of \(335\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\), corresponding to uptake of three \(\mathrm{Zn}\) ions \((\mathrm{Zn}_3\mathrm{C4Q})\) and utilization of six carbonyls. The electrostatic potential (ESP) method was applied to deduce the active sites for \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) storage in C4Q. It is known that the sites with more positive ESP tend to occur in nucleophilic reactions, whereas the more negative ESP areas prefer electrophilic reactions. In other words, the sites with more negative ESP are attractive for discharge reaction as it involves \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) uptake. For the C4Q molecular structure, the carbonyls on and under a molecule display lower ESP than the bilateral carbonyls, suggesting that the \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) ions have strong interaction with two carbonyls at the top and four carbonyls at the bottom. Although the raw quinone compounds are scarcely soluble in water, it should be considered for long-life cycling that the slight solubility of discharge products (e.g., \(\mathrm{Zn}_x\mathrm{C4Q}\)) is accompanied by its possible side reaction on the zinc anode. That is, the dissolved \(\mathrm{C4Q}^{2x-}\) in the electrolyte can pass through the membrane and react with zinc, forming a byproduct on the anode side, which causes the decline of capacity.\(^{45}\) To overcome this issue, a cation-exchange membrane (Nafion) was introduced as a separator to suppress the dissolution of the discharge product and protect the zinc anode from passivation by discharged products, thus leading to excellent cyclic performance. Although these quinones show great promise in aqueous ZIBs, they are still in their infancy, which requires the development of more categories by elaborately designing the molecular structure to achieve higher capacity and voltage in further studies.

Zn Anode in Aqueous ZIBs. Metallic zinc (Zn) has been regarded as an ideal anode material for aqueous batteries because it features high abundance and low cost (ca. USD \(\$2\mathrm{kg}^{-1}\)),[40] low flammability and toxicity, high electrical conductivity, easy processing, and high compatibility and stability in water.[41] Furthermore, the Zn anode possesses a suitable redox potential \((-0.76\mathrm{V}\) vs standard hydrogen electrode (SHE)) in aqueous electrolyte,[36] a high capacity (ca. \(820\mathrm{mA}\) h g\(^{-1}\); 5851 mA h cm\(^{-3}\)),[59] and low polarizability.[25] Early attempts in alkaline electrolyte, however, are somewhat far from practical application owing to the following issues: (1) low plating/stripping Coulombic efficiency of Zn anode;[31] (2) growth of Zn dendrite during cycling;[29] (3) sustained consumption of electrolyte;[23] and (4) formation of irreversible byproducts (e.g., ZnO or \(\mathrm{Zn(OH)}_2\)) on the Zn anode,[126] resulting in severe capacity fading and poor Coulombic efficiency of cell. These drawbacks in alkaline electrolyte system directly drive the recent progress of aqueous ZIBs using neutral or mild acidic aqueous electrolytes.
Although dendritic zinc could be minimized in mild neutral pH (or slightly acidic) electrolyte,[59] the poor reversibility and

low plating/stripping Coulombic efficiency (CE) of Zn anode still challenge its practical application. The main problems associated with the low CE are the consumption of Zn and electrolyte by side reactions, as in the case of the lithium metal anode.[127] Therefore, significant excessive zinc needs to be used to maintain the cycle stability, but this leads to suboptimal utilization of its theoretical capacity. In addition, the regular replenishing of the electrolyte with water was often required to compensate the water decomposition.[23,128] Despite most previous reports that high charge/discharge rates were used to minimize the effect of their poor reversibility,[36,72,129] the goal of achieving high CE in aqueous ZIBs remains a challenge.

To address these issues, many research efforts have been focused on electrolyte optimization; these include the species and concentration of zinc salts, as well as the additive in electrolyte,\(^{31-33,52,106}\) which will be discussed later. It is known that the formation of zinc dendrite and corrosion mainly originate from uneven \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) distribution on the flat or two-dimensional metallic Zn foil. The sharp end of the initial small dendrite tip on traditional planar current collectors\(^{130}\) could serve as a charge center in the electric field for continuous accumulation of charges, thus amplifying the growth of dendrites via further deposition on those sharp ends. In contrast, the hierarchical current collector with high electroactive surface area and uniform electric field could inhibit dendrite formation.\(^{131}\) Therefore, deposition of Zn into hierarchical current collectors may represent a potential strategy to alleviate dendrite growth and achieve high rate and stable ZIBs. For instance, Fan et al. designed a Zn nanoflakes array-coated 3D graphene foam (Zn-GF) anode (Figure 14A),\(^{132}\) in which 3D porous GF acts as a highly conductive substrate and macroporous agent to alleviate the formation of inactive surface oxide or hydroxide layer and dendrite growth. Moreover, this Zn-GF anode possesses higher capacity and smaller polarization (Figure 14B), as well as lower charge-transfer resistance (Figure 14C) than that of conventional compact Zn foil, implying the improved electrochemical activity and fast \(\mathrm{Zn}/\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) redox kinetics of the zinc nanoarray structure. In addition, several significant works also focused on this field, such as Zn-coated carbon cloth anode (Figure 14D),\(^{133}\) Zn-coated carbon nanotube paper anode (Figure 14E),\(^{134}\) Zn-coated carbon nanotube yarns (Figure 14F),\(^{135}\) Zn-coated nitinol yarns,\(^{136}\) etc. These novel Zn-hierarchical current collector anodes greatly diminish the formation of dendrites

Aqueous Electrolytes. Electrolyte plays a vital role in battery chemistry as its functions to transport guest ions and make a connection between cathode and anode. The use of aqueous electrolytes offers many advantages in terms of low cost, operational safety facile manufacture, environmental benignity and high ionic conductivities. Currently, the \(\mathrm{ZnSO_4}\) or \(\mathrm{Zn(CF_3SO_3)_2}\) salt-based electrolytes are considered to be promising electrolytes and widely used in aqueous ZIBs, as shown in Table 1. Many works focus on adjusting the concentration of zinc salts or using the additive to encourage better electrochemical performance for aqueous ZIBs. Chen et al. investigated four mild acidic solutions separately containing zinc salt of \(\mathrm{ZnCl_2}\), \(\mathrm{ZnNO_3}\), \(\mathrm{ZnSO_4}\), and \(\mathrm{Zn(CF_3SO_3)_2}\) in a \(\mathrm{Zn} / \mathrm{ZnMn}_2\mathrm{O}_4\) system. [33] The voltammetry in \(\mathrm{Zn(CF_3SO_3)_2}\) electrolyte (Figure 15A) and \(\mathrm{ZnSO_4}\) electrolyte (Figure 15B) indicates good reversibility of \(\mathrm{Zn}\) stripping/plating for both electrolytes. It also demonstrates a poor CE for \(\mathrm{ZnCl_2}\) and \(\mathrm{ZnNO_3}\) electrolytes associated with the instability of \(\mathrm{Cl^-}\) and \(\mathrm{NO}_3^-\), which is also observed in a \(\mathrm{Zn - V}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) system by Zhou and co-workers. [106] The effect of zinc salt concentration on \(\mathrm{Zn}\) stripping/plating CE was also discussed by Chen et al., who indicated that a high-concentration electrolyte with increased viscosity (Figure 15C) displays better CE than that of low-concentration counterparts. It is supposed that higher salt concentration could not only alter the solvation and transporting behaviors of cations/anions to enhance stability and kinetics [138] but also reduce the water activity and water-induced side reactions. [139] The CE of the \(\mathrm{Zn} / \mathrm{ZnMn}_2\mathrm{O}_4\) cell increases with salt concentration and is almost unchanged (\(\sim 100\%\)) at \(3\mathrm{M}\) (Figure 15D), which enables long-term stability with a capacity retention of \(94\%\) over 500 cycles at \(500\mathrm{mA g^{-1}}\). The additive in the electrolyte plays a vital role in suppressing

dendrite growth, thus improving the Zn stripping/plating CE. This is done by reducing the corrosion on zinc\(^{32}\) or enabling planar zinc deposition.\(^{52}\) For instance, according to Chen et al., the addition of \(\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{SO}_4\) in \(\mathrm{ZnSO}_4\) electrolyte could effectively avoid the growth of Zn dendrites,\(^{52}\) because \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) with a lower reduction potential could form a positively charged electrostatic shield around the initial protuberances of Zn and avoid further dendrite deposition.\(^{140}\) As a result, the case with \(\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{SO}_4\) did not present a large number of vertical and harsh Zn dendrites formed on the surface of Zn anode (Figure 15E,F).
The use of aqueous electrolytes offers many advantages in terms of low cost, operational safety, facile manufacture, environmental benignity, and high ionic conductivities.

Very recently, Wang and colleagues developed a concentrated aqueous electrolyte consisting of both the lithium salt LiTFSI and the zinc salt \((\mathrm{Zn(TFSI)_2})\), where TFSI is bis(trifluoromethane) sulfonamide. Molecular dynamics (MD) studies indicate that in the most concentrated electrolyte \((1\mathrm{M}\mathrm{Zn(TFSI)}_2 / 20\mathrm{M}\) LiTFSI), the \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\)-solvation sheath is occupied primarily by TFSI, with six coordinating oxygens all from TFSI, rather than \(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\) in the most dilute electrolyte \((1\mathrm{M}\mathrm{Zn(TFSI)}_2 / 5\mathrm{M}\) LiTFSI) (Figure 16A). This is very important to suppress the dendrite formation of Zn metal during cycling (Figure 16B), because strong interaction between \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\) and \(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\) can prompt formation of inactive \(\mathrm{ZnO}\) that limits reversibility by causing active material loss and dendrite growth on the Zn metal surface. This unique electrolyte with optimized \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\) solvation-sheath not only enables dendrite-free Zn plating/stripping at nearly \(100\%\) CE

Summary and Perspectives. In this Review, we focus on the recent significant progress of aqueous ZIBs and provide in-depth discussions of main aspects involved in their development. Particularly, the main three energy storage mechanisms in aqueous ZIB systems are summarized: (1) \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) insertion/ extraction reaction mechanism, (2) chemical conversion reaction mechanism, and (3) \(\mathrm{H}^{+}/\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) coininsertion/extraction reaction mechanism. A comprehensive understanding of the battery materials is also proposed. These include the merits and issues related to Zn anode, optimized electrolyte, and various types of cathodes, as well as the optimization strategies aiming at high-performance aqueous ZIBs.

However, many challenges still exist in making great breakthroughs in high-performance aqueous ZIBs for practical applications. Here, the deep insights of potential future research directions will provide a guide for the high-efficiency energy storage as follows:

In-Depth Understanding of Energy Storage Mechanisms. To date, many works have focused on energy storage mechanisms for aqueous ZIB systems; however, the detailed information about the intrinsic electrochemical reaction mechanisms in this

system is still under debate. For one thing, many of the guest ions in this system, such as \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\), \(\mathrm{H^{+}}\), or other cations (e.g., \(\mathrm{Li^{+}}\), \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\), \(\mathrm{Mn^{2+}}\)) from the electrolyte additive, could react with cathode materials; their different insertion thermodynamics and kinetics make it hard to determine the specific reaction. For another thing, various cathode materials all lack available data in terms of theoretical number of guest ions and their sites stored in the host of these cathode materials because of the complexity of reaction and the negative effects from side reactions. In this regard, more powerful tools, such as advanced characterization technologies and precise calculations, are urgently needed for further investigation and prediction to accelerate the understanding of currently unclear reaction mechanism in aqueous ZIBs.

Toward a Highly Reversible Zn Anode. The main issues such as formation of dendritic zinc, corrosion, and poor plating/stripping columbic efficiency still plague the development of the Zn anode. As mentioned above, many research efforts focus on the electrolyte optimization and construction of novel Zn composite anodes; however, the discussion related to the design of novel monolithic Zn anode has rarely been reported. In nickel-zinc alkaline cells, Rolison et al. have designed a 3D monolithic zinc sponge anode that elevates the performance without undergoing passivation or macroscale dendrite

formation.\(^{25}\) Therefore, to make improvements in aqueous ZIBs, these topics, such as construction of a three-dimensional Zn foam or sponge, should also be significantly addressed in future research. Also, motivated by suppressing Li dendrite growth via membranes or interfacial engineering,\(^{130}\) the surface modification of Zn anode aiming to reduce the corrosion on zinc and support planar zinc deposition could be taken into account, but it should be based on the fast Zn plating/stripping.

Novel Strategies for Cathode Materials. It can be seen from the present Review that divalent \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\) ions have strong electrostatic interaction with many cathode materials in aqueous ZIBs, thus causing slow \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\) diffusion. These kinetic issues have also hindered other multivalent battery technologies, such as MIBs and AIBs,[112] which could be addressed by structural engineering and exploring new cathode materials. For structural engineering, because cation vacancy summarized in this Review shows the possibility of unlocking the electrochemical activity of well-known materials,[33] other structural defects (e.g., oxygen vacancy, ion doping, phase boundary, highly active facets, etc.) discussed in other battery systems[141-145] could potentially provide new ideas for future generations of aqueous ZIBs. In addition, cathode materials with features of high porosity, large surface area, and enlarged interplanar crystal spacing may facilitate \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\) diffusion.[146-148]

For exploring new cathode materials, according to the aforementioned discussion, many cathode materials, such as Mn-based oxides and V-based oxides, exhibit complicated electrochemical behaviors with phase transformation or formation of a Zn-containing phase during insertion/extraction of \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\), which may be ascribed to the high electronegativity of oxygen.[117] Therefore, the structures without oxygen (e.g., metal carbides, metal sulfides, and metal nitrides) can serve as an alternative to diminish this effect and are reactive enough to enable fast diffusion of \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\), because these elements have electronegativity that is lower than that of oxygen.
It can be seen from the present Review that divalent \(Zn^{2+}\) ions have strong electrostatic interaction with many cathode materials in aqueous ZIBs, thus causing slow \(Zn^{2+}\) diffusion.

Designing Flexible Aqueous ZIBs. Flexible and wearable batteries may provide high flexibility and reliable electrochemical properties for portable and flexible electronics.[149] Recently, flexible aqueous ZIBs with gel or solid-state electrolyte have shown great potential in this field,[52,56,133-135] because they may solve the intrinsic safety issues of LIBs with organic electrolytes. However, one of the main challenges is how to achieve flexible aqueous ZIBs with high zinc-ion conductivity, which quite depends on the two key components of the ZIBs (namely, electrolyte and electrode). For the electrolyte, the currently used polymer electrolytes (e.g., poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA)-based electrolyte) mainly suffer from low ionic conductivity and poor mechanical strength. Therefore, new strategies are highly desired to achieve a well-designed flexible electrolyte with high zinc ion conductivity and superior mechanical properties for the further development of extremely safe and rechargeable ZIBs. For instance, Zhi et al. introduced a novel polyacrylamide (PAM)-based polymer electrolyte with an ultrahigh ionic conductivity of \(1.76 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~S~cm}^{-1}\), remarkable flexibility, and favorable mechanical strength,[134] suggesting the selection of polymer host is a good choice. For electrodes, they are generally limited by the poor mass diffusion of divalent cation \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) in the bulk lattice; therefore, more attention should be paid to improving the reaction kinetics of \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\). In addition, the designed electrodes should be compatible enough with the polymer electrolyte to reduce the interface barrier of ionic transport.
